common_name: Spider Flower (Garden Cleome, Spider Legs, Hassler's Spider Flower)

planting.note: Direct sow at the soil surface (seeds require light to germinate — do not cover) after last frost in full sun; Spider Flower grows rapidly in warm conditions and tolerates summer heat and drought better than most tall annuals. Self-seeds prolifically, often naturalizing in gardens. Thin to 18–24 inches apart for best development. The sticky, slightly fragrant foliage and stems deter many pests. Excellent for hummingbirds and butterflies; a host plant for some Sphinx moth larvae. Deadheading is not needed — the elongating seed pods below the active flower cluster are ornamental.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Spider Flower grows vigorously in hot, dry, sunny conditions and is one of the most heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant of the tall summer annuals, making it well-suited to Gulf Coast zone 9-11 conditions where many other tall annuals struggle. Tarenaya hassleriana (formerly Cleome hassleriana) is a tall, fast-growing annual native to South America (Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay) in the Cleomaceae family, widely grown throughout the world's warm-climate gardens as one of the finest tall background annuals for hot summer gardens. The plants produce large, rounded, domed clusters of flowers at the tips of their erect, branching stems — each individual flower has four narrow petals in pink, rose, white, or lavender-purple, but the defining feature is the extremely long, thin, prominent stamens (6) and style that project far beyond the petals like the legs of a spider — earning the plant its common name. Below the active flower cluster, the developing seed pods (5–8 inches long, narrow, cylindrical) hang on thread-like stalks, extending the ornamental interest below the flower head. The whole elongating display — active flowers above, pendant seed pods below — creates an unusual, multi-dimensional ornamental effect. The cultivar series 'Queen' (in pink, white, violet, rose, and mixed) is the standard commercial form. Self-seeds abundantly.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cleome hassleriana (Spider Flower) for Florida gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Spider Flower for Gulf Coast zone 9 summer gardens
LSU AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Summer Annuals for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Cleome hassleriana

common_name: Crystal Orchid (Crested Coelogyne, Himalayan White Orchid, Snow Orchid)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct morning light in well-draining bark medium or mounted on wood in a cool location; Coelogyne cristata is a cool-growing Himalayan orchid that requires cool, dry winters (45–55°F nights in fall and winter) to trigger its spectacular late-winter bloom. Without a cool winter rest with reduced watering, it will not bloom. Grow on a sheltered porch where winter temperatures drop to 45–55°F. Water regularly in summer growth; reduce substantially from October through February. The large clusters of pure white, fragrant flowers emerge in January–March. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to filtered direct light in a cool location — Crystal Orchid is a cool-growing orchid that requires genuine winter cool (nights 45–55°F) to initiate its bloom and will not flower in warm indoor conditions year-round, making a cool, sheltered porch in zone 8-10 an ideal environment. Coelogyne cristata is a sympodial, epiphytic orchid native to the Himalayan foothills of Nepal, India (Sikkim, Darjeeling, Assam), and Bhutan at elevations of 3,000–7,000 feet in oak and rhododendron cloud forests in the Orchidaceae family. The plant produces oval, deeply furrowed pseudobulbs in dense clusters, each bearing 2 long, narrow, grass-like leaves. In late winter and early spring (January–March), the plant produces pendant clusters of 5–10 flowers on gently arching racemes — and these flowers are considered among the most beautiful white flowers of any orchid: large (3–4 inches across), pure crystalline white with a finely textured, glistening surface, and a central three-lobed lip with five prominent, raised, orange-yellow crests that give the species its name 'cristata' (crested). The flowers are lightly sweet-fragrant. On a well-established clump in full bloom with dozens of pendant white flower clusters arching from every side, C. cristata is one of the finest sights in the orchid world. Zone 8-10 gardeners with cool winters have an advantage with this species over those in warmer climates. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Coelogyne cristata cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Coelogyne cristata Himalayan species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Coelogyne cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Coelogyne (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Tiger Stanhopea (Downy Stanhopea, Inverted Orchid, Upside-Down Orchid)

planting.note: MUST grow in an open slat basket or mesh basket — NEVER in a solid pot; Stanhopea's flower spikes grow downward and penetrate through the basket bottom to bloom below the plant. A solid pot prevents flowering entirely. Use coarse bark medium. Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun. Water regularly in active growth; reduce slightly in winter. The flowers last only 3–5 days but the fragrance is extraordinary. Bloom season is summer. Protect from temperatures below 50°F. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in a slatted or open-mesh hanging basket in bright indirect to filtered direct light — Tiger Stanhopea MUST be grown in an open-bottom container because its flower spikes emerge from the base of the pseudobulbs and grow downward through the basket medium to bloom below the plant — a pot with a solid bottom prevents flowering. Stanhopea tigrina is a sympodial, epiphytic orchid native to Mexico (Veracruz, Oaxaca, Chiapas, Puebla) in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most fascinating orchids in cultivation for several reasons: its extraordinary downward-blooming habit, its large and complex flowers, and its remarkable fragrance. The plant produces oval pseudobulbs with broad, heavily-veined leaves, and in summer, one to four flower spikes per pseudobulb emerge from the base, push through the bark medium, and emerge hanging downward below the basket — where they open simultaneously into 3–8 of the most bizarre and dramatic flowers in the orchid family. Each flower is large (4–5 inches), waxy, and complex in shape: the sepals and petals are pale yellow heavily spotted and blotched with maroon-purple spots (the 'tigrina' — tiger-spotted), with a sculptural, two-part, boat and hook-shaped lip. The fragrance is powerful, complex, and sweet-spicy (vanilla, cinnamon, and eucalyptus are described), used by male Eulaema bees to attract females. Flowers last 3–5 days. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Stanhopea tigrina: growing the upside-down orchid
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Stanhopea tigrina species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Stanhopea cultivation: basket requirements
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Stanhopea (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Five-Ribbed Gongora (Quinquerib Gongora, Punch Orchid, Incense Orchid)

planting.note: Grow in an open slat basket or mounted on cork — like Stanhopea, Gongora produces pendant (downward-hanging) flower racemes that must emerge from the bottom of the container; a solid pot prevents blooming. Use coarse bark or mount without medium. Bright indirect to filtered direct light. Water regularly in active growth; reduce in winter. Blooms in summer with cascading pendant spikes of fragrant flowers. Fast-growing and free-blooming once established. Protect from temperatures below 50°F. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in an open-bottom slatted basket or mounted on cork in bright indirect to filtered direct light — Five-Ribbed Gongora produces its pendant flower spikes downward through the basket medium and cannot bloom in a solid container. Gongora quinquenervis is a sympodial, epiphytic orchid with a wide natural range from Mexico through Central America to Peru, Bolivia, Brazil, and Venezuela in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most ornamentally rewarding of the pendant-blooming orchids for several reasons: it is fast-growing, free-blooming (producing multiple long pendant racemes per pseudobulb annually), and produces its flower display more persistently than the typically brief-flowering Stanhopeas. The plant forms dense clusters of ribbed, angular pseudobulbs each with 2 broad, pleated leaves. In summer, multiple flower spikes (6–18 inches) emerge from the base of the pseudobulbs and cascade downward in long, graceful arcs bearing 15–30 flowers each — the individual flowers are about 1 inch across, highly complex in shape (insect-mimic form with the column modified to match Eulaema bee proportions), typically in yellow-orange to tawny-orange with reddish-brown spots and a contorted, complex lip. The flowers emit a warm, sweet, incense-like to honey-anise fragrance used by Eulaema male bees to attract females. A cascade of multiple pendant Gongora spikes in summer bloom is a graceful and unusually beautiful display. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Gongora quinquenervis cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Gongora quinquenervis species profile
Missouri Botanical Garden — Gongora: pendant-blooming tropical orchids
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Gongora (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Queen of the Andes (Titanka, Giant Puya, Raimondi's Puya)

planting.note: Grow in full sun in very well-drained, poor to moderately fertile, rocky or gravelly soil; Puya raimondii is a slow-growing Andean bromeliad that tolerates cold (to 15–20°F), heat, drought, and poor soils. It will not tolerate waterlogged or humid subtropical conditions — it is adapted to the cold, dry, sunny, high-altitude grasslands (puna) of the Andes at 10,000–15,000 feet elevation. In zone 7-9 with dry conditions, it can be grown as a specimen plant over many decades, eventually producing the largest bromeliad flower spike in the world after 80–150 years of vegetative growth. A plant of extraordinary ecological and botanical significance — the largest bromeliad on Earth. Monocarpic (dies after flowering).

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, poor, rocky or gravelly soil — Queen of the Andes requires the extremely well-drained, dry, nutrient-poor conditions of its native high-altitude Andean habitat and will rot in any soil that retains moisture for extended periods or in humid subtropical conditions. Puya raimondii is an extraordinary, slow-growing, rosette-forming terrestrial bromeliad native to the high-altitude grasslands (puna) of Peru and Bolivia in the Bromeliaceae family — the largest bromeliad species on Earth, and one of the most remarkable plants in all of biology. The plant spends 80–150 years as a vegetative rosette of stiff, spine-tipped, sword-like leaves 3–6 feet long with upward-curved, sharp, hook-shaped marginal spines, forming a hemispherical mound 5–10 feet across. Then, once in its lifetime, it produces the largest flower spike of any bromeliad on Earth: a towering, erect, densely-branched panicle 25–35 feet tall bearing 8,000–10,000 individual white flowers simultaneously — the floral display visible from miles away in its native Andean habitat. After the flowering — which may take 3–4 months — the entire plant dies (monocarpic behavior), producing thousands of seeds that fall from the massive spike. The spike structure provides critical habitat for Andean birds and insects during bloom. At lower elevations, growth is slower and even less likely to reach blooming size, but the rosette alone — a dramatic, architectural, spine-armed mound — is an extraordinary garden specimen for decades.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Puya raimondii: Queen of the Andes
Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History — Puya raimondii ecology and biology
Desert Botanical Garden — Puya raimondii cultivation in North American gardens
Missouri Botanical Garden — Puya raimondii species profile
